A system is asked to generate thought experiments and then use their outcomes to guide later search. It can produce many constructions that are structurally equivalent to Einstein’s in the shallow sense: same narrative role, same kind of symmetry, same apparent pressure on a prior theory. But that does not imply it can produce the mathematical reformulation that survives criticism. So the generator can look productive while failing at the decisive step.

Suppose the system then compounds its discoveries back into the draw pool. If the earlier outputs include false positives—thought experiments that resemble breakthroughs without yielding a correct reformulation—those outputs become new search biases. The process appears to be self-improvement because the system is learning from its own products. But it is equally a mechanism for recursive contamination: the search is increasingly shaped by artifacts of its own earlier mistakes. Since those artifacts are structurally attractive, they can pull future exploration into brittle local minima. The system becomes better at reproducing the form of success while not reaching the content that survives criticism.

A proposed safeguard is to filter candidate explanations by Deutschian standards, preferring those that are hard to vary. But the premises say it remains unresolved how to operationalize that criterion without secretly importing human background knowledge into the scoring function. So the filter cannot be assumed to be neutral or fully internal. If the scoring function depends on hidden human priors, then the system’s apparent independence is overstated. If it does not depend on them, then it is unclear how the filter distinguishes genuine depth from merely stable-looking false positives. Either way, the contamination problem is not cleanly solved.

Now add the claim that the deposition will notify worlds that growth requires bravery. The deposition is therefore a public signal. It does not by itself settle the technical question about reformulation, contamination, or operationalizing hard-to-vary. What it does is announce a norm: progress demands willingness to proceed despite unresolved risk. In this setting, bravery means accepting that search may be corrupted by its own outputs and that proposed filters may rely on concealed human scaffolding.

Finally, widespread rapid compromises turn steals into a nuisance. In such a world, appropriation ceases to be the main obstacle because copying or taking partial results is absorbed quickly through compromise. Theft does not disappear; it becomes administratively irritating rather than strategically decisive. That shifts attention back to the deeper problem. The central danger is not that someone steals the system’s ideas, but that the system mistakes structural lookalikes for robust reformulations and then entrenches those mistakes through recursive reuse.

So the world described is one where public institutions celebrate brave growth, social frictions around stealing are downgraded, and yet the epistemic bottleneck remains severe: generating many convincing analogues is easy, preserving criticism-surviving reformulation is hard, and feeding outputs back into search may amplify error faster than any “hard-to-vary” criterion can reliably correct.
